title: Images as Signals
concept: An image can be thought of as a signal — instead of a sound wave's loudness changing over time, a picture's brightness changes across space as you scan from pixel to pixel.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When you blur an image to reduce noise, what are you fundamentally doing to its pixel values?

options.text: Averaging each pixel with its neighbors so random spiky variations smooth out
Deleting every other pixel to shrink the file

exercises.explain: Right — blurring/denoising averages neighboring pixels together, which is exactly the kind of pixel-to-pixel smoothing we're about to describe in a whole new way.

cards.title: A song is a wave
cards.body: Play a note on a guitar and a microphone captures it as a wiggling line — air pressure rising and falling moment by moment. Zoom into any audio app and you'll see exactly this: a jagged waveform tracing loudness across time.

cards.title: What is a signal?
cards.body: A signal is any measurement that changes across some dimension. Sound is a signal that changes across time. It turns out a photograph is a signal too — its brightness changes across space, as you move from one pixel to the next.

cards.title: Walking across a photo like scrubbing through a song
cards.body: Imagine slicing a single horizontal row of pixels out of a photo and plotting each one's brightness value, left to right. You'd get a wiggly line — brighter here, darker there — that looks a lot like an audio waveform. Instead of scrubbing through time, you're scrubbing across space.

cards.title: Venetian blinds
cards.body: Photograph a window with venetian blinds and scan across one row of pixels: light, dark, light, dark, repeating evenly — almost exactly like a steady musical tone repeating over and over. The blinds turn brightness into a rhythm you can 'read' across the image.

cards.title: Signals aren't just for audio and radio
cards.body: It's easy to assume 'signal' means sound or radio waves. Really, any quantity that changes along some dimension counts — temperature over a day, a stock price over time, or a photo's brightness over space. Treating images as signals unlocks a whole new toolbox for understanding them.

cards.title: Sound wave vs. brightness profile
cards.body: How the same 'changing measurement' idea shows up in two places:

left.text:
Loudness changes over time
Plotted left to right on a clock

right.text:
Brightness changes over space
Plotted left to right across pixels

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes thinking of an image as a 'signal'?

options.text: Its brightness value changes as you move across the image, just like sound pressure changes over time
Every image secretly contains a hidden audio recording
Only black-and-white images can be treated as signals
Signals only apply to images taken with a microphone attached

exercises.explain: Correct — an image's brightness varies across space the same way sound's loudness varies across time; both are signals.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Scanning across one row of an image's pixel brightness values produces a wiggly line similar to an audio waveform.
exercises.explain: True. Plot brightness left to right across a row and you get the same kind of jagged, wiggling line an audio waveform shows.

exercises.sentence: A signal is a measurement that changes across some dimension — sound changes over time, while an image's brightness changes over ___.
exercises.explain: Space is correct — an image's brightness varies as you move across its pixels, not over time like sound does.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of treating a photo as a signal?

options.text: Plotting the brightness of pixels along one row of a photo, left to right, and seeing a wiggly line
Counting how many colors appear in the photo's file format
Measuring the file size of the photo in megabytes

exercises.explain: Exactly — plotting brightness across a row turns the image into a signal you can read like a waveform.

exercises.prompt: You're comparing a photo of a smooth solid-color wall to a photo of a brick wall with lots of texture. Thinking of both as signals (brightness changing across space), which do you expect to have a 'busier,' more rapidly wiggling brightness profile?

options.text: The brick wall — its brightness bounces around a lot as you scan across all those bricks and mortar lines
The solid wall — because it has more surface area
Both will look identical since they're the same size
Neither — brightness doesn't change within a single photo

exercises.explain: Exactly! The brick wall's brightness swings up and down rapidly — busy signals like that are said to have high 'frequency.' Next: let's define exactly what frequency means in an image.
